content.problemStatement:
Today, Chef woke up to find that he had no clean socks. Doing laundry is such a turn-off for Chef, that in such a situation, he always buys new socks instead of cleaning the old dirty ones. He arrived at the fashion store with **money** rupees in his pocket and started looking for socks. Everything looked good, but then Chef saw a new jacket which cost **jacketCost** rupees. The jacket was so nice that he could not stop himself from buying it.


Interestingly, the shop only stocks one kind of socks, enabling them to take the unsual route of selling single socks, instead of the more common way of selling in pairs. Each of the socks costs **sockCost** rupees.



Chef bought as many socks as he could with his remaining money. It's guaranteed that the shop has more socks than Chef can buy. But now, he is interested in the question: will there be a day when he will have only 1 clean sock, if he uses a pair of socks each day starting tommorow? If such an unlucky day exists, output "Unlucky Chef", otherwise output "Lucky Chef". Remember that Chef never cleans or reuses any socks used once.
content.inputFormat: The first line of input contains three integers — jacketCost, sockCost, and money — denoting the cost of a jacket, cost of a single sock, and the initial amount of money Chef has, respectively.
content.outputFormat: In a single line, output "Unlucky Chef" if such a day exists. Otherwise, output "Lucky Chef".

sampleTestCases.explanation: After buying the jacket, Chef has 2 rupees left, which is enough to buy only 1 sock. Therefore, there will be a day when he has only one clean sock left.
After buying the jacket, Chef has 5 rupees left, which is enough to buy 2 socks (for 4 rupees). Therefore, he will always have an even number of socks and never have only one clean sock.

solution.approach: To determine if Chef will have an unlucky day, we first calculate how many socks he can buy after purchasing the jacket. If the total number of socks is odd, then there will be a day when he has only one clean sock left. Otherwise, he will always have an even number of socks and thus never have only one clean sock.
solution.timeComplexity: O(1) — the time complexity is constant because we only perform a fixed number of operations regardless of the input size.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — the space complexity is constant because we use a fixed amount of space to store the input and the result.

code.Python3:
jacketCost, sockCost, money = map(int, input().split())
remainingMoney = money - jacketCost
numSocks = remainingMoney // sockCost
if numSocks % 2 == 1:
    print("Unlucky Chef")
else:
    print("Lucky Chef")
